Sam Deen: I'm unable to find more observations but I think the photometry is bad on the (807) and (695) data. Another object discovered adjacently in the exact same search is 2002 GS31, which was observed by (807) as having R=20.5. The full photometry on GS31 shows that it was closer to R=22.0 instead. That's not just an issue with these asteroids though - very old Kitt Peak and Mayall telescope observations from pre-2005 had a very bad habit of massively overestimating the brightness of many objects including TNOs by up to 3 magnitudes for whatever reason. Anyway, I'm unable to find it in NEAT images from 2002/03/12 down to V=21 which adds additional credence to that. Applying the same correction to the 695/807 measurements of GR31 the asteroid was closer to V=22.0 at the time.

Sam Deen: I think whoever measured this at M58 did a very bad job of calibrating their magnitudes, which is why it seems unusually bright. The only clear image I was able to get from DECam was i=21.8, which is about 1 full magnitude fainter than their average magnitude or about H=18.5.
